coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on dual-emitting carbon dots for the rapid detection of sulfite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Mingyan
Mi, Wenxing
Guo, Xiaowei
Zhang, Mei
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) derivatives are typically employed as antioxidants in food and pharmaceutical processing. However, excessive sulfite intake could trigger serious health problems. Hence, it is urgent to establish a rapid and effective system for monitoring SO2. This study adopted a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize dual-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CECDs) and developed a ratiometric sensor for sulfite using CECDs-Cr (VI) composites. The emission intensity ratio (I440/I500) of the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites increased considerably with the addition of HSO3 −. A method based on the ratiometric sensor was established for SO2 derivatives with advanced efficiency and excellent linearity over a broad concentration range of 0–500 μM (R 2 = 0.9946). Four medicine-food homology materials (MFHMs) fumigated with sulfur have been accurately detected using this approach. Furthermore, a portable test tube was prepared to achieve rapid and semi-quantitative detection of SO2 residues and applied to real samples. This work presents an effective approach to develop a rapid on-site detection platform for sulfite residues in food and pharmaceuticals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric sensor
Sulfur dioxide
Test tube
Medicine-food homology materials

Portable test tube for the determination of SO2
Fluorescent response of CECDs-Cr (VI) to sulfite
Determination of SO2 residues in MFHMs
A portable test tube for SO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CECDs; (b) particle size distribution of CECDs; (c) XPS full spectrum of CECDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1 s; (e) O 1 s; (f) N 1 s.

Fluorescence spectra of CECDs-Cr (VI) obtained at (a) 340 nm and (b) 430 nm excitation wavelengths with increased HSO3 − concentrations. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CECDs, CECDs-Cr (VI), Cr (VI), Cr (VI) + HSO3 −, Cr (III) and CECDs-Cr (VI) + HSO3 −. (d) Images of solutions of Cr (VI), Cr (VI) + HSO3 −, and Cr (III) in the absence and presence of EDTA.

Influence of (a) time and (b-c) pH on the determination of HSO3 − by the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites. (Ri is the I440/I500 of the CECDs-Cr (VI) + HSO3 − and R0 refers to the I440/I500 of the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites).

(a) Linear fitting relationship between I440/I500 and HSO3 − concentrations (0–500 μM); (b) Effect of various interferents (200 μM) on the I440/I500 in the absence and presence of HSO3 − (200 μM). I440 and I500 refer to the maximum emission wavelength when the λex = 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively.

(a) Schematic illustration of the device; (b) Images of test strips under 365 nm UV light after exposure to increasing concentrations of HSO3 −; (c) Linear relationship between the luminosity values of images and HSO3 − concentrations.
Determination of SO2 residues in MFHMs (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The Chinese government limits SO2 residues in Angelicae dahuricae Radix to less than 150 mg/kg and those in the other three MFHMs to less than 400 mg/kg.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on dual-emitting carbon dots for the rapid detection of sulfite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources, School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources
Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources, School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) derivatives are typically employed as antioxidants in food and pharmaceutical processing. However, excessive sulfite intake could trigger serious health problems. Hence, it is urgent to establish a rapid and effective system for monitoring SO2. This study adopted a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize dual-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CECDs) and developed a ratiometric sensor for sulfite using CECDs-Cr (VI) composites. The emission intensity ratio (I440/I500) of the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites increased considerably with the addition of HSO3 −. A method based on the ratiometric sensor was established for SO2 derivatives with advanced efficiency and excellent linearity over a broad concentration range of 0–500 μM (R 2 = 0.9946). Four medicine-food homology materials (MFHMs) fumigated with sulfur have been accurately detected using this approach. Furthermore, a portable test tube was prepared to achieve rapid and semi-quantitative detection of SO2 residues and applied to real samples. This work presents an effective approach to develop a rapid on-site detection platform for sulfite residues in food and pharmaceuticals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) derivatives (i.e., sulfites and bisulfites) are commonly utilized in the processing and preservation of food, beverages, and pharmaceuticals owing to their dehumidifying, pest control, antioxidant and antibiotic properties [1,2]. Although endogenous SO2 is involved in many physiological and pathological activities of humans, excessive sulfite intake could trigger serious health problems, such as cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, allergies, and even lung and brain tumors [3–6]. As a result, sulfite levels in food, beverages, and drugs have been restricted strictly worldwide [7,8]. Developing a fast and efficient way for monitoring SO2 derivatives in real samples is imperative.

Currently, a variety of methods including titration [9], colorimetry [10], electrochemistry [11], and chromatography [12–14] are used to determine SO2 and its derivatives. However, the time-consuming procedures, expensive equipment, and complex sample pretreatment hinder their use in rapid detection. In contrast, the fluorescence method is a good candidate for rapid detection owing to its merits of simple operation and high sensitivity. It has successfully detected various toxic and harmful substances and biomarkers [15–18]. Nevertheless, fluorescent sensors that rely solely on a single fluorescent intensity for sensing are prone to inevitable signal fluctuations resulting from probe concentration fluctuations, co-existing interferents, instrumental efficiency and environmental conditions [19,20]. To increase the accuracy of the analysis, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor can provide a good solution by introducing another fluorescence variable as an internal reference. Using the fluorescence intensity ratio at two wavelengths as the response signal can significantly reduce systematic errors and expand the valid measurable range, resulting in more reliable data [21–23]. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescence measurement is quite satisfactory for accurately and rapidly analyzing sulfite.

Fluorescent materials typically include organic small molecules, quantum dots, metal–organic frameworks, upconversion nanoprobes, metal nanoclusters, and green fluorescent protein [19,24–27]. However, most of these fluorescent materials have limitations, such as complicated preparation procedures, low water solubility, and poor biocompatibility. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials, which possess some unique optical and chemical properties, including simple preparation, low cost, low toxicity, high fluorescence yield, good water solubility and good biological compatibility [4,5,28]. These superior properties make CDs outstanding fluorometric sensors for diverse analytes, like cells [29], pH [30], metal ions [31], and small molecules [32].

Herein, citric acid and ethylenediamine-synthesized carbon dots (CECDs) were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method for the SO2 derivatives analysis in real samples by ratiometric fluorometry. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization results indicated that CECDs were uniformly spherical morphology with a mean diameter of 2.4 nm and nitrogen was successfully doped in it. The CECDs solution had dual emission centers positioned at 440 and 500 nm, respectively. A ratiometric fluorescence sensor for SO2 derivatives was designed based on CECDs-Cr (VI) composites. HSO3 − could enhance the fluorescence of the sensor at 440 nm by inhibiting the inner filter effect (IFE), yet quench the fluorescence at 500 nm, thereby markedly increasing the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I500). Consequently, we developed a method for SO2 derivatives sensing among four medicine-food homology materials (MFHMs) based on the ratiometric fluorescent sensor, which showed satisfactory linearity and recoveries. Moreover, the SO2 in MFHMs was also semi-quantified by a disposable test tube developed based on CECDs-Cr (VI) composites.

Na2HPO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2C2O4, CH3COONH4, Na2S, Na2SO4 Na2S2O3, Na2S2O8, NaHSO3, NaNO3, NaF, NaCl, KBr, KI, CuCl2, NaOH, K2Cr2O7, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), ethylenediamine, citric acid, ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), L-Cysteine (L-Cys), quinine sulfate and fluorescein have been acquired from Aladdin Reagents Co. All chemicals were analytically pure without further purification.

The transmission electron microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer employed for the sample characterization were from JEOL Co., Ltd. JEM-2100F and the Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250xi, respectively. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra and emission spectra were recorded on a Lengguang Technology Co. model F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer employed to examine the absorption spectra is the TU-1901 from Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CECDs and preparation of CECDs-Cr (VI) composites
ce:section.ce:para: CECDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method based on previous work by making several modifications [33]. Dissolve citric acid (1.0507 g) and ethylenediamine (335 µL) in ultrapure water (10 mL), then transfer this solution into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. It is subsequently heated to 200 °C for 5 h and cooled to room temperature. Then the resulting mixture was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min) and the large particles were separated through a microporous filter (0.22 μm). To further purify the CECDs solution, a 48-hour dialysis process was continued using a dialysis kit (1000 kDa molecular weight cut off) in distilled water. The CECDs solution was dried to obtain a brown powder. Finally, CECDs (2.5 μg/mL)-Cr (VI) (300 μM) composites were obtained by adding K2Cr2O7 to the CECDs solution for the following experiments.

The quantum yields (QY) of CECDs were determined upon excitation at 340 nm and 430 nm with quinine sulfate (0.05 M H2SO4 as solvent) and fluorescein (0.1 M NaOH as solvent) as reference substances, respectively. The calculation is as follows [34]:
Φ u
=
Φ s
×
F u
×
A s
×
n u 2
F s
×
A u
×
n s 2
where Ф means QY. F means the integral of the fluorescence intensity. A means the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. n means the refractive index of the solvent. u and s correspond to CECDs and quinine sulfate (340 nm)/fluorescein (430 nm), respectively.

The buffer solution (pH 5.0) used for subsequent experiments was citric acid (0.1 M)-Na2HPO4 (0.2 M). The three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectrum of CECDs (2.5 μg/mL) was first measured. For the fluorescence titration, the fluorescence spectra of CECDs (2.5 μg/mL)-Cr (VI) (300 μM) composites were obtained upon the addition of increasing concentrations of HSO3 − (0–1000 μM) with excitation wavelengths (λex) at 340 nm and 430 nm in turn. All the above experiments were carried out in triplicate to make sure the results were stable and reliable. To exclude the interference of certain reducing substances, the pH of the solution was adjusted to around 9.0 with NaOH (0.1 M). Subsequently, 300 μM of Cu2+ was added, not only to form a high stable CuS precipitate (Ksp = 6.36 × 10−36) [35], but also to catalyze the oxidation of AA [36]. The same method was used to exclude the interference of thiols.

The SO2 in MFHM samples was extracted using an acid distillation method based on the Chinese Pharmacopoeia [37] with some modifications. Four commercially available MFHM samples, including Dioscoreae Rhizome, Gastrodia Rhizoma, Angelicae dahuricae Radix, and Codonopsis Radix were ground into fine powders. 10.0 g of each MFHM powder was put into a custom-made flask, and then 300–400 mL of water and 10 mL of HCl solution (6 M) were added sequentially. Nitrogen was then introduced to the flask. At the same time, an air guide tube was connected and inserted into a conical flask with 50 mL of water. Finally, the round bottle flask was heated for 1.5 h. The obtained SO2 absorption solution was sealed and stored away from light. To detect the SO2 residue in the MFHM samples, the diluted absorbent solution was added into the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites solution and the emission spectra were measured at λex = 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The spiked recovery test was performed by adding 120, 240 and 360 μM of HSO3 − to the detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable test tube for the determination of SO2
ce:section.ce:para: A portable test tube incorporating test strips was designed for rapid detection of SO2 residues. The 1 × 1 cm2 filter paper piece was rapidly soaked in CECDs solution for 1 s. After drying, it was further rapidly soaked in Cr (VI) solution for 1 s. After being air-dried, the test strip was prepared. Following that, the test strip was fixed to the inside of the cap of a 5 mL centrifuge tube to obtain a disposable test tube. Then, the test tubes were filled with 2 ml of HSO3 − solutions with concentrations of 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 mM, respectively, followed by the addition of a drop of HCl solution to each. After the test tubes were heated for 10 min under 60 ℃, the test strips were dried and photographed in a UV dark box analyzer at 365 nm. The parameters of the camera are a shutter speed of 1/15 s, and ISO of 6400. Adobe Photoshop was used to analyze the histogram of each image of the test strip. A linear fitting relationship was established between the luminosity corresponding to 0.299*R+0.587*G+0.114*B (for sRGB) and HSO3 − concentrations. To determine SO2 residues in real samples, Codonopsis Radix and Angelicae dahuricae Radix were ground into fine powders. 0.5 g of each sample powder was introduced to the test tube containing 2 mL of water directly, followed by the addition of a drop of HCl solution to each. Then, the test tubes were heated and the test strips were imaged using the same procedure as with the standard HSO3 − solution.

In this paper, we synthesized CECDs using the one-step hydrothermal method. TEM and XPS were used to characterize the morphology, elemental composition, and functional groups of CECD. TEM images (Fig. 1 a) showed that CECDs were spherical and uniformly distributed with particle sizes ranging from 1.3 to 3.8 nm. Their average size was 2.4 nm (Fig. 1b). According to the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c), the CECDs were composed of carbon (67.44 %), nitrogen (17.85 %) and oxygen (14.71 %). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of CECDs, the peak of C 1 s was decomposed into three parts, 284.80 eV, 286.10 eV, and 287.79 eV (Fig. 1d), corresponding to the C–C/C=C, C=O/C-OH and C-N bands, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum can be fitted to two peaks of 531.21 eV and 532.38 eV (Fig. 1e), proving that O was mainly presented as O=C and HO-C. The N 1 s spectrum had two distinct peaks at 399.69 eV and 400.46 eV (Fig. 1f), which were attributed to N–H and N-C bonds, respectively. Besides, the quantum yields of CECDs were measured. The QY was calculated to be 7.53 % at λex = 340 nm using quinine sulfate as a reference and 20.23 % at λex = 430 nm using fluorescein as a standard. The high quantum yields ensure that CECDs can emit strong fluorescence even at low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of CECDs-Cr (VI) to sulfite
ce:section.ce:para: The 3D fluorescence spectrum of CECDs revealed two emission centers positioned at (λex, λem) = (340, 440) nm and (430, 500) nm, respectively (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Materials). The dual-emitting property endows CECDs with the potential to be used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe. With the introduction of Cr (VI), the λem = 440 nm was significantly quenched and the λem = 500 nm was partially quenched (Fig. S2). Correspondingly, the QYs of CECDs decreased from 7.53 % to 4.83 % at λex = 340 nm and from 20.23 % to 6.45 % at λex = 430 nm. By comparing the absorption spectrum of Cr (VI) (Fig. 2 c) and the 3D fluorescence spectrum of CECDs, it was observed that Cr (VI) had a strong absorption band around 340 nm which overlapped with the excitation band of CECDs at 340 nm, while Cr (VI) had a weak absorption band around 430 nm. Thus, the significant fluorescence quenching of CECDs at 440 nm was attributed to the IFE caused by Cr (VI). In the presence of HSO3 −, the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites underwent dramatic changes in emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 2a–b, the intensity of fluorescence at 440 nm increased, while the intensity of fluorescence at 500 nm decreased. According to these findings, the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites show promise to be used as ratiometric fluorescence sensors for sulfite detection.

We supposed that the significant increase of emission intensity at 440 nm was ascribed to the redox reaction that takes place between Cr (VI) and HSO3 −. The valence of Cr ion was reduced from six to three, which suppressed the Cr (VI)-caused IFE and restored the fluorescence of CECDs. To prove our assumption, UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 2c, upon the addition of HSO3 − to Cr (VI), the resulting absorption spectrum exhibited coincidence to that of Cr (III), which lacks discernible absorption bands. Even in the coexistence of CECDs, the spectral change of Cr (VI) upon the addition of HSO3 − remained consistent with the aforementioned observations. The generation of Cr (III) was confirmed through a chromogenic reaction upon the introduction of EDTA. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the Cr (III)-EDTA complex solution exhibited a purple coloration. Upon the addition of EDTA, the Cr (VI)-HSO3 − solution exhibited a purple coloration as well, indicating the conversion of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) [38]. The decrease of emission intensity at 500 nm was ascribed to the cooperative interaction of Cr (VI)/Cr (III) and HSO3 −, given that the addition of either Cr (III) or HSO3 − alone did not alter the emission spectra of CECDs (Fig. S3). In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of CECDs at 500 nm were measured before and after the addition of Cr (VI) and HSO3 −. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence lifetime exhibited minimal alteration (τBNCDs = 6.35 ns, τBNCDs-Cr (VI) = 6.12 ns, τCECDs-Cr (VI) + HSO3 −=6.06 ns), suggesting the occurrence of static fluorescence quenching.

To obtain better detection performance, a series of reaction conditions were optimized. Firstly, the Cr (VI) concentration was optimized (Fig. S5). The results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I500) of CECDs-Cr (VI) exhibited the most pronounced change when the Cr (VI) concentration was 300 μM. So, the optimal Cr (VI) concentration was selected as 300 μM for further experiments. Subsequently, we added Cr (VI) and HSO3 − successively into the CECDs solution to evaluate the variation of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I500) over time. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the I440/I500 remained nearly unchanged 10 min after the addition of Cr (VI) to the CECDs solution. After the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites were stabilized, HSO3 − was added. The I440/I500 of the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites reached a maximum within 10 min. To ensure their full integration and stable results, the optimal response time was chosen as 20 min. Finally, the reaction pH was optimized. The ratios (I440/I500) of CECDs-Cr (VI) and CECDs-Cr (VI) + HSO3 − were tested respectively after stabilizing for 20 min under various pH conditions (pH 2.2, 3, 4, 5, 6). As demonstrated in Fig. 3b–c, upon the addition of HSO3 −, the ratio (I440/I500) and its change (Ri/R0) of the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites reached their maximum at pH 5.0. Thus, pH 5.0 was taken as the optimal pH. In short, the optimal Cr (VI) concentration, response time and pH were determined to be 300 μM, 20 min and 5.0, respectively.

Good linearity and selectivity are the preconditions of the CECDs-Cr (VI) sensor-based analytical method. We first evaluate the linearity between I440/I500 and HSO3 − concentration. Satisfactorily, a superior linear correlation was observed across a broad concentration range from 0-500 μM (R 2 = 0.9946) (Fig. 4 a). The emission intensity at 440 nm exhibited a slight change, while that at 500 nm demonstrated a notable decrease when a low concentration of HSO3 − was introduced. As the HSO3 − concentration increased, the signal at 500 nm rapidly reached saturation, while the signal at 440 nm continued to increase significantly (Fig. 2a–b). The differential response sensitivity at the two wavelengths ensured the wide linear range between the emission intensity ratio (I440/I500) and the HSO3 − concentration. In addition, the detection limit was calculated to be 2.92 μM according to three times of the standard deviation of the blank solution (3σ/s). As listed in Table S1, compared with recent reports on CDs for HSO3 −, the CECDs-Cr (VI) sensor-based analytical method has excellent performance with a wider linear range and a comparable LOD. In addition, CECDs can be easily synthesized with high QY and possess ratiometric properties, making them a promising candidate for use in portable and rapid visual detection of HSO3 −.

Then, the selectivity of CECDs-Cr (VI) composites for HSO3 − was assessed. A series of interferents such as F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, NO3 −, AcO−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, C2O4 2−, S2−, AA, L-Cys and GSH were tested. Although the direct addition of some certain reducing substances can interfere, we can eliminate their interference by Cu2+ in advance (Fig. S6). After masking them, the CECDs-Cr (VI) system showed almost no response to these interferents, as displayed in Fig. 4b. Even in the presence of other coexisting interferents, the response of CECDs-Cr (VI) to HSO3 − was not affected. The above results indicated that the CECDs-Cr (VI) sensor-based analytical method has good linearity and selectivity toward HSO3 − and can be utilized for detecting HSO3 − in complex actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SO2 residues in MFHMs
A portable test tube for SO2
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve rapid on-site detection of SO2 residues, a portable test tube was developed. Given the toxicity of Cr (VI), CECDs-Cr (VI) was immobilized on the filter paper to minimize the usage of Cr (VI) and the risk of leakage into the environment. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the test strip containing the CECDs-Cr (VI) composites was attached inside the lid of the plastic centrifuge tube to obtain the test tube. Upon the addition of HCl and heating of the tube, the residual SO2 in the sample will volatilize and react with the test strip. The test strips were recorded under a 365 nm UV lamp after different contents of HSO3 − were evaporated. As Fig. 5b showed, the luminosity of the test strips gradually increased with increasing concentrations of HSO3 −. A good linear fitting relationship between luminosity and HSO3 − concentration was observed (R2＞0.97) (Fig. 5c). Based on these desirable results, we applied the device to detect SO2 residues in real samples to verify its semi-quantitative capability. By comparing the luminosity of the test strips, the sulfite concentrations in Codonopsis Radix and Angelicae dahuricae Radix were estimated to be between 0–1 mM (0–256 mg/kg for SO2), and the SO2 residue in Codonopsis Radix is higher than that in Angelicae dahuricae Radix (Fig. S7). The values are in accordance with those obtained from the titration method. These results suggest that the test tube-based method has good semi-quantitative ability for SO2, which is favorable for the fast on-site determination of SO2 residues in real samples.

To sum up, we developed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on dual-emitting CECDs-Cr (VI) composites to detect SO2 residues in MFHMs. CECDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method and a ratiometric CECDs-Cr (VI) sensor was obtained after the introduction of Cr (VI). The existence of HSO3 − could enhance the fluorescence of the sensor at 440 nm by inhibiting the IFE, while quenching the fluorescence at 500 nm. A CECDs-Cr (VI) sensor-based method was developed for the rapid detection of HSO3 −. Under optimum experimental parameters, a well-defined linear correlation was obtained between the emission intensity ratio (I440/I500) and the concentration of HSO3 − ranging from 0 to 500 μM (R 2 = 0.9946). The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was effectively applied to the detection of SO2 residues in four MFHMs with satisfactory recovery results. Furthermore, a portable test tube was prepared for the on-site semi-quantitative determination of SO2 residues in MFHMs quickly. This work offers an effective approach to develop a simple and fast on-site detection platform for sulfite residues in food and pharmaceuticals.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82374000, 82174273); Xinglin Scholar Research Promotion Project of Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine (QJRC2021009); Sichuan Province Science and Technology Activities Funding for Returned Overseas Scholars.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125132.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blueberry fruit quality and control of blueberry maggot (Rhagoletis mendax Curran) larvae after fumigation with sulfur dioxide

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of chitosan coating combined with sulfur dioxide fumigation on the storage quality of fresh areca nut

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanocluster-based colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-mode sensor for the detection of bromide and sulfite in waters and wastewaters

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on coumarin dye-functionalized carbon dots for bisulfite detection in living cells and food samples

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A wide-range ratiometric sensor mediating fluorescence and scattering based on carbon dots/metal-organic framework composites for the detection of bisulfite/sulfite in sugar

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.J. Wang, Z.Y. Xu, Z. Sun, Z.Q. Li, Y.H. Luo, H.Q. Luo, N.B. Chem., 415 (2023) 4639-4647.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of aldehyde-functionalized dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of bisulfite in food samples
